draft.introduction: Matthew’s resurrection account includes an earthquake, an angel descending, and the Great Commission: “Go and make disciples of all nations.” The trinitarian baptismal formula appears here for the first time.

translation.text: Now after the Sabbath, toward the dawn of the first day of the week, Mary Magdalene and the other Mary went to see the tomb. And behold, there was a great earthquake, for an angel of the Lord descended from heaven and came and rolled back the stone. His appearance was like lightning. The angel said to the women, “Do not be afraid, for I know that you seek Jesus who was crucified. He is not here, for he has risen, as he said.”
translation.original_text: οὐκ ἔστιν ὡδε ἠγέρθη γὰρ καθὼς εἶπεν

translation.text: And Jesus came and said to them, “All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you. And behold, I am with you always, to the end of the age.”
translation.original_text: πορευθέντες οὖν μαθητεύσατε πάντα τὰ ἔθνη βαπτίζοντες αὐτοὺς εἰς τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ πατρὸς καὶ τοῦ υἱοῦ καὶ τοῦ ἁγίου πνεύματος

translator_notes.note: The trinitarian formula—“in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit”—is the only place in the Gospels where all three persons are named together in a liturgical formula. Some scholars question its authenticity, noting that Acts records baptism “in the name of Jesus” only.
